Afghanistan is often seen only through conflict, but there is another side of the country that is deeply human, peaceful, and unforgettable. Thanks you.

comments.author: @Earthcue
comments.text: Afghanistan has changed its national flag 26 TIMES — more than any other country in the world! 🚩 Since 1709, every shift in power brought a new flag, from kingdoms to republics to empires. Each flag tells a chapter of one of the most turbulent stories in human history.

comments.text: I come from Afghanistan, but I have been living in Europe for a long time. I have written two interesting books: Afghanistan – The Invisible Trap and Where Afghanistan Died in Me. Both are available on Amazon in German and English.

comments.author: @Dukhtare-Kabul1
comments.text:
As an educated Afghan woman who has been forced to live in Germany since 2019 because of insecurity, war, and economic poverty in Afghanistan, this documentary made me cry for my country, for my people, and for myself.
I cried for the air and atmosphere of my homeland that I may not be able to experience again anytime soon. I cried for a country with so much beauty and economic potential, yet destroyed and left behind in so many ways because of dirty politics and endless war.

My heart truly broke while watching it, but at the same time, I felt proud that people around the world could finally see Afghanistan from another perspective — not only through the lens of war and politics. 🇦🇫♥️

Please, whoever reads my comment, as a human being, try not to forget the girls and women of my homeland. Afghan girls deserve to go to school and university. Afghan women deserve to work and live with dignity. They truly need support, and sadly, my people and my country have been deeply forgotte. ❤🇦🇫 @PlanetVista4K Thank you for focusing on the beauty of my country and for showing the world what Afghanistan has always hidden within itself. 🙏🏻🙏🏻

comments.author: @samonirama3040
comments.text: Oh my God, this is the first documentary I've seen about my country. It's so beautiful. I'm really proud of my country. Even though I've been away from my country for 25 years, today I saw this movie and thought I was living in my country.❤️❤️❤️

comments.author: @maiwndmaqsood
comments.text: Afghanistan’s history is a tapestry of empires from the Achaemenids to the Silk Road's heartbeat in Balkh and Herat, where scholars, poets, and artists once lit the world. Its culture sings through centuries: Rumi’s echoes, miniature paintings, intricate carpets woven with stories. And hospitality? It’s sacred. A stranger becomes family over shared bread and chai, no questions asked. Amid all this beauty blue mosaics of Mazar-i-Sharif, amber light on the Hindu Kush, and faces etched with quiet grace Afghanistan remains a poem the world has yet to fully read, but one that knows how to welcome every traveler with an open heart.

comments.author: @WaisM3
comments.text: I regret that such an interesting documentary about the Afghanistan landscape is half mixed with images that were not from Afghanistan or are misplaced. Also, the word Persian is completely and wrongly interpreted by Western scholars, whereas Persian civilisation did end with the invasion of Alexander the Great in the 4th century BC and with the fall of the Sasanian Empire to the Arab Caliphate in the 7th century.
